_meta.what_firm_does: Figma is a collaborative, browser-based design platform where teams design, prototype, and build together in real time. Mission: make design accessible to everyone.
_meta.jd_wants: Build and deliver enablement that drives product adoption across a customer portfolio — read customer data and goals, design tailored enablement plans, deliver training at scale, and partner cross-functionally to identify champions and expansion.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: Enablement isn't just about running a training—from my experience, it's about reading what a stakeholder's data and goals are telling you, designing the plan that meets them where they are, and refining it through collaboration until adoption genuinely takes hold. That full cycle is the work I do now. At InGenius Prep I design enablement plans and documentation across 20+ programs, deliver customized live training and webinars for 600+ participants, and run needs assessments with 25 global stakeholders to inform how I engage each one. A Customer Enablement Manager role is that instinct aimed at a product portfolio—tailoring enablement to where each customer actually is, then proving adoption moved. That's exactly the kind of problem I like owning end-to-end.
Why do you want to work at Figma?: The mission resonates—making design accessible to everyone, not just specialists. That democratizing instinct mirrors how I think about enablement: my job is to lower the barrier so people can actually get value from a tool, not just access it. And the role fits how I work. I build relationships across functions—at InGenius I partner across Marketing, Tech, and Sales—and I analyze metrics and feedback to spot champions and expansion opportunities, which is the heart of portfolio enablement. I'm drawn to the idea that real enablement isn't a one-time training; it's a relationship you tend with data. Figma feels like a place where that craft is taken seriously.
What excites you about this opportunity?: Tailoring enablement to a portfolio's maturity—meeting an early customer differently than an established one—is the part I find genuinely interesting, because it's a diagnosis problem before it's a delivery problem. At Vestas I optimized onboarding across three global locations by analyzing where the experience actually broke down and clarifying responsibilities, building trust with leaders along the way. Doing that across a customer portfolio at Figma, where adoption directly shapes whether teams keep designing together, is energizing. I also like that it rewards measurement: not just 'did they attend,' but 'did usage and value actually grow.' Building lasting adoption, and being able to show it, is what I'd get up for.
At InGenius Prep, product understanding was uneven across teams, so adoption of our programs and tools was inconsistent. I diagnosed where people were getting stuck through needs assessments with 25 global stakeholders, then designed enablement plans, guides, and customized live training for 600+ participants tailored to each group's starting point. The result was a 78% program enrollment rate and onboarding time cut by 75%. What made it work was treating adoption as a cycle, not an event—I tracked metrics and feedback to find champions and kept refining the plan. That's the throughline I'd bring to Figma: diagnose where users struggle, tailor enablement to where they are, and keep measuring until adoption genuinely holds.
